3A_Bibliographic.citation: Woo, J., Shin, J., & Seo, S. (2023). Negative impact of daily screen use on inhibitory control network in preadolescence: A two-year follow-up study. Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience, 61, 101218.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: The final sample size after removing missing values was 8324 for the baseline year (interview date: 09/2016-10/2018) and 3891 for the year 2 follow-up (interview date: 07/2018-01/2020).
3B_Sample_Data.notes: Baseline n=8324 for cross-sectional; n=3891 for longitudinal follow-up analyses.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Screen-Activity Proportion (SAP) score: ratio of daily screen time to total daily screen time plus activity time
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey (STQ, abcd_stq01) and Sports and Activities Involvement Questionnaire (abcd_spacss01)
3C_Exposure.modalities: total screen time (TV shows/movies, videos, video games)
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous proportion score (SAP = Daily Screen Time / (Daily Screen Time + Daily Activity Time))
3C_Exposure.reporter: Self-report (youth) for screen time; Parent-report for activities
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: SAP score = Daily Screen Time / (Daily Screen Time + Daily Activity Time). The Screen-Activity Proportion (SAP) score reflects how much individuals are exposed to the screen on a daily basis compared to non-screen activities.
3C_Exposure.notes: Novel composite measure combining screen time and physical activity.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Fronto-striatal (frontoparietal network to striatum) resting-state functional connectivity
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: ABCD rsfMRI Network to Subcortical ROI Correlations (mrirscor02) - Gordon parcellation FPN to bilateral caudate, putamen, accumbens
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: the values representing the strength of the frontoparietal network (FPN) to striatum connectivity were generated by averaging the connectivity values of the FPN to bilateral caudate, FPN to bilateral putamen, and FPN to bilateral accumbens.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Robust correlation with 50,000 permutation resampling and Bayesian hypothesis testing; bootstrapping mediation analysis
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: sex, age, household income, parental education level
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Data analyses were performed by combining a 50,000 permutation resampling method and the robust method... The statistical results from both Frequentist (i.e., p-value) and Bayesian approaches were reported.
3E_Design_Analysis.notes: Uses both frequentist and Bayesian approaches. Covariates included in supplementary analyses.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Fisher z-transformed correlation of FPN-striatum connectivity
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: a significant negative association between the Screen-Activity Proportion (SAP) and the fronto-striatal connectivity in the baseline year (r = -0.040, 95% CI 50,000 bootstrap = [-0.0605, -0.0186], BF10 = 13.10)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Fisher z-transformed correlation of FPN-striatum connectivity
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: a stronger and significantly negative association between the SAP and the fronto-striatal connectivity than with the baseline neural data (r = -0.0933, 95% CI 50,000 bootstrap = [-0.1231, -0.0635], BF10 = 428995.74)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Change in fronto-striatal connectivity (year 2 minus baseline)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: the daily screen exposure time during the baseline year was found to predict changes in connectivity strength between the baseline and year 2 (year 2 minus baseline) (r = -0.0340, 95% CI 50,000 bootstrap = [-0.0645, -0.0035], BF10 = 0.349)

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: High SAP group vs Low SAP group (baseline)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Cohen's d for group comparison of FPN-striatum connectivity
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: children with longer screen time had significantly lower fronto-striatal connectivity, compared to children with shorter screen time (Mhigh = 0.021, Mlow = 0.027, independent t-test: t(1571) = 3.043, p = 0.002, Cohen's d = 0.153, BF10 = 5.487)

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: High SAP group vs Low SAP group (year 2)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Cohen's d for group comparison of FPN-striatum connectivity

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: SAP (mediator: BAS score -> SAP -> fronto-striatal connectivity year 2)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: the SAP significantly mediated the effect between the BAS score and the year 2 fronto-striatal connectivity (indirect effect: B = -6.920e-5, SE = 1.504e-5, p < 0.001, 95% CI 5000 bootstrap = [-1.006e-4, -4.303e-5])

3G_Reporting_Quality.large_n_verbatim: the small effect size of the present study could be due to the target behavior (i.e., daily screen use), as it is expected that the effect of daily behavior is smaller compared to the effect of addictive behaviors... Bayesian approaches are able to evaluate the relative plausibility of the alternative hypothesis (H1) and the null hypothesis (H0) at the same time, which can avoid the inflated false positive rate due to the large sample size (i.e., more than 10,000 in the ABCD data)
3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: Due to the heterogeneous nature of the neural data in the ABCD study, the effect sizes represented by the correlation coefficient in the current study are relatively small, particularly for the baseline year. However, it has been reported that the true size of brain-behavior effects is smaller than what has been previously reported and anticipated... the present study suggests that the impact of daily screen use may be growing, although it was relatively small to begin with.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: The paper acknowledges small effect sizes (r = -0.040 at baseline) and discusses the large sample inflating false positives via Bayesian approach. However, it then argues effects are growing over time and frames findings as evidence of negative neural impact.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Given that the virtual movement is irreversible and expanding in the modern and future life, it is imperative to conduct more research on the impact of daily screen exposure.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion draws strong parallels between screen time effects and substance addiction, comparing fronto-striatal connectivity patterns to those seen in smoking, alcohol dependence, and cocaine addiction. Despite acknowledging small effect sizes (r = -0.04 to -0.09), the authors argue these effects may be growing over time and frame screen use as having 'a similar effect to that of habitual addictive seeking behaviors.' The language shifts from correlational findings to causal-sounding claims about screen time 'delaying' brain development.
